common_name: Mystic Spires Blue Salvia (Long-Spiked Deep Blue Proven Winners Hybrid Salvia)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Mystic Spires Blue is a sterile hybrid that does not set seed — reproduction is by division or purchase of new plants. Deadheading spent spikes encourages rapid rebloom and maintains the neat appearance of the plant.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Mystic Spires Blue is a Proven Winners hybrid salvia (Salvia longispicata x S. farinacea) that has become one of the most popular and widely sold salvias in American nurseries — and for good reason. The flower spikes are extraordinary: 12–18 inches long, densely packed with deep cobalt-blue to deep blue-violet flowers, held stiffly upright on branching stems for a display that far exceeds the spike length of standard mealy sage varieties. The extended spikes and prolific branching create a dramatic blue mass effect when established plants are in full bloom — a visual impact that has made Mystic Spires Blue one of the premier blue-flowered annuals and tender perennials for Gulf South summer and fall borders. The sterile hybrid status means no reseeding and no escaping to natural areas — an environmental advantage over some self-seeding sage species. The bloom season runs from spring through hard frost in zone 9, with deadheading of spent spikes promoting rapid, continuous new spike production. Unlike many salvias that slow or pause in peak summer heat, Mystic Spires maintains reasonable bloom production even in July–August in zone 9. In zone 8-9, established plants may survive mild winters and return as perennials; in zone 10-11, they are true perennials.

common_name: Coral Bells Azalea (Classic Coral-Pink Masses-of-Bloom Kurume Azalea)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5) soil in partial shade; Kurume azaleas perform best in morning sun with afternoon shade protection in Gulf South climates — afternoon shade reduces heat and drought stress during the critical summer period.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5) soil in partial shade; Coral Bells is a classic Kurume azalea cultivar — one of the Japanese Kurume (city of origin, Kurume, Japan) hybrids that are among the most widely planted azaleas in the American South, characterized by small, glossy leaves, compact mounding habit, and spring flowering so profuse that the foliage can be completely obscured by flowers. The spring bloom display of 'Coral Bells' — masses of small, hose-in-hose (double) to single, coral-pink to warm rose-pink flowers covering the entire plant simultaneously — is one of the quintessential signs of Gulf South spring alongside dogwood and wisteria. The hose-in-hose flower form (one flower nestled inside another, giving the appearance of a double flower with extra petals) is characteristic of many Kurume cultivars and adds ornamental complexity to each individual flower. In zone 9, Kurume azaleas perform as reliable evergreen foundation and landscape shrubs that require only occasional pruning after bloom to maintain their compact, mounded habit. The small, glossy, dark-green leaves provide attractive year-round structure. Strongly acidic, humus-rich soil and consistent moisture are the most critical requirements — chlorosis from insufficient acidity is the most common cultural problem, corrected by soil acidification with sulfur and acid-forming fertilizers.

common_name: Hino Crimson Azalea (Brilliant Red Small-Leaf Compact Kurume Azalea)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5) soil in partial shade; Hino Crimson's bright red flower color is most vivid with morning sun and afternoon shade — full afternoon sun can cause the red color to fade slightly and increases water stress in Gulf South summers.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5) soil in partial shade; Hino Crimson is a Kurume azalea cultivar producing one of the most vivid red flower displays of any small-leaf azalea — masses of brilliant, intense red single flowers in spring that cover the plant completely at peak bloom, creating a display significantly more vividly red than the pink-to-pink-red tones of most other azaleas. The very small leaves (typical Kurume fine texture) and compact, mounded habit make Hino Crimson one of the most refined and formal-looking of the spring-blooming azaleas — a tight, dense, naturally rounded shrub that requires minimal pruning to maintain its shape. The cold-hardiness of Hino Crimson (to zone 5) exceeds that of Southern Indica and most other azalea groups, making it adaptable to a broader geographic range, including the cooler portions of zone 9 where indica types can be borderline. In Gulf South zone 9, Hino Crimson is a reliable, long-lived evergreen foundation shrub that blooms dependably each March–April. The intense red color makes it particularly striking in mass plantings along walks and driveways where the simultaneous red bloom creates a powerful seasonal display. Planted where early morning sun can backlight the flowers, the vivid red becomes almost incandescent — one of the most spectacular light effects achievable in the spring garden.

common_name: High Fragrance Camellia (Exceptionally Fragrant Blush-Pink Hybrid Camellia)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) soil in partial shade; High Fragrance is a hybrid between C. japonica and C. lutchuensis — the C. lutchuensis parent contributes the fragrance that is absent in standard C. japonica varieties.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) soil in partial shade; High Fragrance is one of the most important fragrant camellia cultivars — a hybrid between Camellia japonica and the fragrant species C. lutchuensis that produces the elegant, refined flowers of japonica combined with a genuine sweet, jasmine-like fragrance that is absent from the vast majority of camellia varieties. Most camellia flowers, despite their beauty, are essentially scentless — the discovery that crossing with the fragrant C. lutchuensis could introduce lasting fragrance into hybrid offspring was a significant horticultural development. High Fragrance produces medium-sized, semi-double to peony-form flowers in soft blush-pink to white-with-pink tones in late winter to early spring — the same season as other camellias — with a sweet, clean fragrance detectable several feet from the plant under favorable conditions. The fragrance intensity is variable with temperature: warm, calm mornings amplify the scent; cold or windy conditions reduce it. The plant grows as a medium-sized, evergreen shrub with the characteristic glossy, dark-green camellia foliage. An outstanding choice for planting near entryways, patios, and garden seating areas where the fragrance can be appreciated. In zone 9, High Fragrance is a reliable perennial shrub that blooms most heavily in late January–March.

common_name: Olympian Fig (Exceptionally Cold-Hardy Brown-Skinned Productive Fig)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Olympian is one of the most cold-hardy fig cultivars — suitable for zone 6-7 gardens where most other figs are marginal, and exceptionally reliable in the Gulf South where cold hardiness is rarely a limiting factor.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Olympian is a fig cultivar noted for exceptional cold-hardiness and reliable productivity — it is one of the hardiest named figs commercially available, surviving temperatures well below those that damage other cultivars, making it the preferred choice for gardeners at the northern edge of fig culture (zone 6-7) and an extremely reliable fruiting tree throughout zone 8-10. The medium to large fruits have brown to brown-violet skin, amber flesh with reddish tones, and a pleasant sweetness — not as dramatically flavored as Violette de Bordeaux but consistently good and productive. Olympian typically produces a breba (early summer) crop on previous year's wood in addition to the main crop on current year's wood, extending the harvest season. The cold-hardiness advantage of Olympian is most significant in zone 9a and northward, where occasional cold snaps below 20°F would damage less-hardy cultivars; in zone 9b-10, it provides a comfortable margin of cold tolerance safety. Self-fertile, like all common figs, and a vigorous, productive tree that begins bearing in its second or third year from planting. The large, distinctive lobed leaves of all figs provide attractive tropical-looking foliage through the growing season. Figs produce two crops in warm climates: the early breba crop in June and the main crop in late summer through fall.
